foundation.title: STRING
foundation.subtitle: Textual Objects
foundation.content: In Java, Strings are actually objects. They are immutable—once a String is created 'in memory', it can never be changed. Any 'edit' creates a new String entirely.
foundation.scenario: A Physical Stamp. You stamp 'S' on a paper. To change it to 'B', you can't erase it (Immutability). You must get a new piece of paper and stamp a new word.

examples.title: The String Pool
examples.explanation: Java stores strings in a special 'Pool' memory to save RAM.

examples.title: The Null Check
examples.explanation: Always remember a String can be null (EMPTY) in Java—this causes crashes.

examples.title: Equality Logic
examples.explanation: Never use == for strings! Use .equals() to check the content inside.

foundation.keyPoint: Always use .equals() for content comparison, not ==.

foundation.title: METHODS
foundation.subtitle: The Object Tools
foundation.content: String class provides built-in methods (tools) like .length(), .toUpperCase(), and .substring() to manipulate text safely.
foundation.scenario: A Swiss Army Knife. The String comes with its own box of tools built-in. You don't build a 'length' tool; you just ask the string for it.

examples.title: Length Reveal
examples.explanation: Calling a method to count the characters (including spaces) inside.

examples.title: Case Shift
examples.explanation: Creating a new version of the string that is all capitalized.

examples.title: Char At
examples.explanation: Pulling out exactly one character at a specific index like 0 for the first.

foundation.keyPoint: s.method() calls a tool on the string.

foundation.title: SUBSTRING
foundation.subtitle: String Cutting
foundation.content: substring() extracts a part of a larger string. You specify the start index (inclusive) and end index (exclusive).
foundation.scenario: A Slice of Pizza. You have one large pizza (The Full String). You take a slice from 1 to 4 to give to a friend (The Substring).

examples.title: Simple Cut
examples.explanation: Starting at index 2 and taking everything until the very end.

examples.title: Precise Cut
examples.explanation: Taking a slice from index 0 to index 3 (index 3 is NOT included).

examples.title: Empty Slices
examples.explanation: Trying to slice outside the string boundaries (e.g. 0 to 100) causes a crash.

foundation.keyPoint: s.substring(start, end). End belongs to the NEXT slice.

foundation.title: METHOD_DEF
foundation.subtitle: Building Blueprints
foundation.content: In Java, functions are called 'Methods'. They live inside classes and must specify exactly what data they return (int, String, etc.) or 'void' (nothing).
foundation.scenario: A Coffee Machine blueprint. Before building, you must specify what goes in (Beans/Water) and exactly what comes out (Coffee).

examples.title: The Void Method
examples.explanation: A task that performs a job (like printing) but gives NO data back.

examples.title: The Returner
examples.explanation: A method that calculates a result and hands it back to the program.

examples.title: Static Power
examples.explanation: A method that belongs to the class itself, not just one specific object.

foundation.keyPoint: Must declare return type. void = no return value.

foundation.title: PARAMS
foundation.subtitle: The Parameters
foundation.content: Methods take parameters. Java is strictly typed, so you must define the type (e.g. String name) for every input.
foundation.scenario: An Electric Oven. The 'temp' is a parameter. You set it to 200 (Argument) to bake, or 50 to warm. You can only give it numbers (Type).

examples.title: Single Input
examples.explanation: Defining a name for the data going into the method.

examples.title: Multi-Input
examples.explanation: Taking multiple pieces of information separated by commas.

examples.title: Final Results
examples.explanation: Using a parameter inside a logic calculation.

foundation.keyPoint: Java Parameters = Type + Name. Required match.

gameData.q: Correct way to check string equality?
